scenarioName: Pulmonary Embolism — Post-Operative Sudden Dyspnea, S1Q3T3, Supportive Care and Recognition

scenarioStory.history: 52-year-old female, 5 days post-op from a laparoscopic total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy for fibroids. She has been mostly resting in a recliner since discharge, getting up only to use the bathroom and eat. Her surgeon's instructions called for short walks every 2 hours, but with the abdominal soreness she has been less active than recommended. About 25 minutes ago she stood up from the couch to walk to the kitchen and was immediately struck with severe shortness of breath she describes as 'like the air just wouldn't come in.' She felt her heart racing, became lightheaded, sat back down hard, and developed a sharp right-sided chest pain that intensifies with deep inspiration ('I can feel where it hurts when I breathe in'). Her husband noticed her lips were 'dusky' and called 911. Her right calf has felt 'a little tight and warm' for the past day, which she dismissed as 'getting old.' She is a former smoker (quit 4 years ago, 20 pack-years) and takes no estrogen-containing medications. No personal or family history of clotting disorders that she knows of.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 52-year-old woman 5 days post-op (laparoscopic hysterectomy) in obvious respiratory distress with classic PE risk factors: recent surgery, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco. Sudden-onset dyspnea, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, sinus tachycardia at 122, BP 96/62, SpO2 86% on room air, and a unilaterally tender/warm right calf. Lung exam is CLEAR — no rales, no wheeze, no crackles — which is itself a clue (severe hypoxia with clear lungs and risk factors for VTE = PE until proven otherwise). 12-lead shows sinus tachycardia with the classic S1Q3T3 pattern and an incomplete RBBB suggesting right-heart strain. The teaching point is recognition of PE pattern, supportive oxygenation, cautious fluid resuscitation, and rapid transport to a CTPA-capable ED — NOT nitroglycerin, NOT diuretics, NOT bronchodilators. → Supportive O2 + cautious bolus + rapid transport: Crew applies high-flow O2 via NRB at 15 L/min; SpO2 rises to 92%. A small 250 mL NS test bolus is run to support RV preload (since the patient is hypotensive but preload-dependent). Norepinephrine drip is prepared in standby. The receiving ED is notified of high-suspicion PE for rapid CTPA. Transport is initiated upright-of-comfort with continuous monitoring; vitals reassessed q5 min en route. → Stable on supportive care; transport to CTPA ED: Patient transported to a tertiary ED capable of CTPA, anticoagulation/thrombolysis, and interventional radiology. SpO2 maintained 92–95% on NRB. Norepinephrine remains in standby — never started — because the cautious bolus held the MAP. Continuous monitoring includes pulse ox, EtCO2, rhythm, and serial BPs every 5 minutes. Bedside handoff includes risk factors (post-op, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco), exam findings (clear lungs with hypoxia, right calf signs), 12-lead findings (S1Q3T3 + incomplete RBBB), and the rationale for each action withheld (no nitro, no albuterol, no diuretic).

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden, severe shortness of breath that hit her like a wall about 25 minutes ago when she stood up from the couch to walk to the kitchen, with sharp right-sided chest pain that worsens with each breath.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 52-year-old woman 5 days post-op (laparoscopic hysterectomy) in obvious respiratory distress with classic PE risk factors: recent surgery, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco. Sudden-onset dyspnea, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, sinus tachycardia at 122, BP 96/62, SpO2 86% on room air, and a unilaterally tender/warm right calf. Lung exam is CLEAR — no rales, no wheeze, no crackles — which is itself a clue (severe hypoxia with clear lungs and risk factors for VTE = PE until proven otherwise). 12-lead shows sinus tachycardia with the classic S1Q3T3 pattern and an incomplete RBBB suggesting right-heart strain. The teaching point is recognition of PE pattern, supportive oxygenation, cautious fluid resuscitation, and rapid transport to a CTPA-capable ED — NOT nitroglycerin, NOT diuretics, NOT bronchodilators.
relatedChecklist: Recognize PE pattern: post-op, immobility, sudden dyspnea, clear lungs, calf findings
High-flow O2 via NRB; titrate to SpO2 94–98%; escalate to BVM-assisted if WOB worsens
12-lead EKG; identify S1Q3T3 / RBBB pattern of right-heart strain
IV access ×2; cardiac monitor; SpO2 + EtCO2 (low EtCO2 with tachypnea = poor perfusion / V/Q mismatch)
Examine calves bilaterally — unilateral swelling/warmth/tenderness suggests DVT source
Withhold NTG (preload-dependent — RV will collapse); withhold albuterol (no bronchospasm)

scenarioEvents.name: Supportive O2 + cautious bolus + rapid transport
scenarioEvents.description: Crew applies high-flow O2 via NRB at 15 L/min; SpO2 rises to 92%. A small 250 mL NS test bolus is run to support RV preload (since the patient is hypotensive but preload-dependent). Norepinephrine drip is prepared in standby. The receiving ED is notified of high-suspicion PE for rapid CTPA. Transport is initiated upright-of-comfort with continuous monitoring; vitals reassessed q5 min en route.
relatedChecklist: 250 mL NS test bolus; reassess lungs after — STOP if rales develop
Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min ready in standby; start if MAP <65 after bolus
Advance notify ED: 'High-suspicion PE, post-op, S1Q3T3, hypoxic — prep CTPA'
Transport in position of comfort (upright); minimize patient-driven activity (no walking)
Reassess vitals + lung exam + EtCO2 trend q5 min; document trajectory for ED

scenarioEvents.name: Stable on supportive care; transport to CTPA ED
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported to a tertiary ED capable of CTPA, anticoagulation/thrombolysis, and interventional radiology. SpO2 maintained 92–95% on NRB. Norepinephrine remains in standby — never started — because the cautious bolus held the MAP. Continuous monitoring includes pulse ox, EtCO2, rhythm, and serial BPs every 5 minutes. Bedside handoff includes risk factors (post-op, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco), exam findings (clear lungs with hypoxia, right calf signs), 12-lead findings (S1Q3T3 + incomplete RBBB), and the rationale for each action withheld (no nitro, no albuterol, no diuretic).
relatedChecklist: Watch for sudden hypotension, syncope, or rhythm change — RV decompensation warning
Defib pads on; sync mode pre-armed; ready for VT/VF or PEA from RV failure
Bedside handoff: risk factors, S1Q3T3, withheld treatments, response to bolus + O2

scenarioEvents.name: Nitroglycerin given for 'chest pain'
scenarioEvents.description: Crew anchored on the words 'chest pain' from triage and gave SL nitroglycerin 0.4 mg before fully working through the differential. This patient's right ventricle is failing under acute pressure overload from the embolus and is critically preload-dependent — venodilation from nitrates dropped preload, the RV decompensated, and BP collapsed within 90 seconds. Patient becomes lightheaded, BP falls to 64/40, mentation clouds, and pulse becomes thready. The teaching point is that 'chest pain' is not a free pass to NTG — pleuritic pain with hypoxia, clear lungs, post-op risk factors, and right-heart strain on EKG should defer nitrates pending further workup. Crew now must support preload aggressively (cautious bolus + early norepinephrine), maintain oxygenation, and consider push-dose epi as a bridge while the drip is prepared.
relatedChecklist: 500 mL NS pressure bolus; reassess MAP — preload support post-nitro
Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) bridge while norepi drip primed
Start norepinephrine 5–10 mcg/min (≈0.07–0.13 mcg/kg/min for 75 kg) titrate MAP >65
BVM-assisted ventilations supplementing patient's own efforts; FiO2 100%
Document anchoring error; debrief: 'pleuritic pain ≠ nitro' rule for PE pattern

Defib pads on; sync mode pre-armed; ready for VT/VF or PEA from RV failure
Bedside handoff: risk factors, S1Q3T3, withheld treatments, response to bolus + O2
